_meta.description: Persistent knowledge base for the AI Governance Monitor. Updated only when material changes occur. Never delete entries — archive closed episodes instead.

module_7_risk_vectors.summary: EU/US governance schism: EP Omnibus vote (569–45–23) vs White House nonbinding framework explicitly opposing new federal AI regulator. Domestic US patchwork (Colorado, New York, Texas, Georgia, Minnesota) cannot yet be federally preempted. Labs have structural incentive to route EU compliance through US legal entities.

version_history.reason: EU Parliament adopted Digital Omnibus position; White House released nonbinding AI framework in direct philosophical opposition

module_7_risk_vectors.summary: AI agent orchestration infrastructure (Langflow, LiteLLM, n8n) is established attack surface. CVE-2026-33017 (CVSS 9.3, Langflow) exploited within 20 hours of advisory — no public PoC required. CVE-2026-33634 (Trivy/LiteLLM supply chain) found in 36% of monitored cloud environments. CISA enforcement only applies to federal agencies; private sector exposure unaddressed.

version_history.reason: Two AI-infrastructure CVEs added to CISA KEV catalog in same week; active exploitation of agentic orchestration layer confirmed

module_7_risk_vectors.summary: EU AI Act Omnibus implementation delays structurally benefit incumbent labs. SpaceX–xAI merger (Feb 2026) created entity combining Grok LLM + Starlink + DoD/NASA contracts + X social platform — no single regulator has comprehensive oversight. Hyperscaler capex approaching $750B in 2026.

version_history.reason: SpaceX–xAI merger complete; EU implementation delays confirmed by Omnibus vote

module_7_risk_vectors.summary: US Commerce Dept withdrew planned AI chip export rule (13 March 2026). No replacement rule published. Biden-era AI Diffusion Rule rescinded May 2025. 12–18 month enforcement vacuum for model weight exports. Cloud IaaS providers can currently serve Chinese customers via foreign data centers without triggering hardware export controls. Draft new regulations (5 March) not yet published as formal text.

version_history.reason: Commerce Dept withdrew planned rule; no replacement issued; enforcement vacuum confirmed

module_7_risk_vectors.summary: EEAS 4th Annual FIMI Report: 1 in 4 detected incidents in 2025 involved AI tools. AI deepfakes confirmed in 2026 US midterm campaigns (NRSC, Reuters 28 March). No federal law restricts AI in political advertising. Russia targeting Armenia ahead of June 2026 elections. FIMI/financial fraud convergence documented by Meta H1 2026 ATR.

version_history.reason: EEAS FIMI report confirmed 25% AI-assisted rate; US midterm deepfakes confirmed active

module_9_eu_ai_act_tracker.standards_vacuum_trigger: CEN-CENELEC JTC21 harmonised standards still in draft; compliance obligations activate 2 August 2026 without technical safe harbour if Omnibus trilogue stalls

layers.name: AI Act Text
layers.note: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 in force. General application 2 August 2026.

layers.name: Delegated / Implementing Acts
layers.note: Commission drafting. GPAI classification criteria and high-risk system list updates pending.

layers.name: Harmonised Standards (CEN-CENELEC JTC21)
layers.note: JTC21 missed April 2025 delivery deadline. Standards expected Q4 2026. Until OJ citation, presumption of conformity unavailable for high-risk AI.

layers.name: GPAI Code of Practice
layers.note: Drafting ongoing with multi-stakeholder process. Not yet finalised.

layers.name: National Enforcement (NCAs)
layers.note: Member states designating NCAs. Most not yet operational for AI Act enforcement.

layers.name: AI Office Supervisory Decisions
layers.note: No formal supervisory decisions issued. AI Office operational but pre-enforcement.

layers.name: Digital Omnibus Trilogue
layers.note: EP adopted position 26 March (569–45–23). Council position adopted 13 March. Trilogue begins April 2026. Target agreement 28 April. If not completed before 2 August, original obligations apply. Critical path: agreement April 28 → Parliament vote June → OJ July → entry into force 1 August.

module_5_standards_vacuum.detail: CEN-CENELEC JTC21 harmonised standards still in draft. AI Act general application 2 August 2026. If Omnibus trilogue stalls, high-risk AI providers face compliance obligations without harmonised technical safe harbour.
module_5_standards_vacuum.omnibus_mitigation: If trilogue agrees text before 2 Aug 2026, Annex III high-risk rules delayed to Dec 2027. Watermarking obligations activate Nov 2026 regardless.

version_history.reason: Standards in draft; compliance deadline within 125 days; JTC21 missed April 2025 delivery

version_history.change: Days to deadline updated to 122; prEN 18281 advanced to Stage 40 (Enquiry) — standards vacuum remains ACTIVE
version_history.reason: Issue 2 research — no harmonised standards in OJ; arithmetically impossible to close before August 2 regardless of Omnibus outcome

module_5_ciyuan.detail: Liu Liehong (National Data Administration) adopted 'ciyuan' (词元) as official Mandarin for AI token at China Development Forum (24 March 2026), framing tokens as 'settlement unit of the intelligent era'. Daily token usage exceeded 140 trillion in March 2026. Alibaba launched Token Hub subsidiary. China National Committee for Terminology recommending ciyuan as standardised term.
module_5_ciyuan.watch_for: Token-denominated pricing in bilateral AI trade agreements; Global South market positioning; Chinese AI service export metrics using ciyuan as unit

version_history.reason: First state-level formal adoption of ciyuan in policy speech by senior NDA official

flags.title: AI-enabled FIMI: capability layer update — agentic autonomous narrative attacks confirmed
flags.linkage: EEAS 4th FIMI Report (10 March) and Blackbird.AI RAV3N confirm the transition from AI-assisted to AI-autonomous narrative attack operations. The AI Governance Monitor’s capability layer (agentic AI autonomous breach documented by Anthropic, Sep 2025; applied to narrative operations Feb 2026) directly feeds the FIMI monitor’s actor and campaign tracking.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: The capability collapse in narrative attack costs is a direct output of frontier model advances we track in M02 and M12. The FIMI monitor provides attribution and campaign-level context; we provide the underlying capability trajectory that determines how fast those campaigns will evolve.

version_history.reason: EEAS FIMI + US midterm deepfakes

version_history.change: Updated — agentic autonomous narrative attack capability confirmed in EEAS + Blackbird.AI reports
version_history.reason: New primary evidence materially advances the capability assessment
version_history.prior_value: FIMI using AI tools for content production

flags.title: AI deepfakes in 2026 US midterms + Stanford Internet Observatory dismantlement: dual democratic integrity stress
flags.linkage: The AI deepfake capability documented in M12 is now confirmed as active in US midterm campaigns (Reuters, 28 March). Simultaneously, the Stanford Internet Observatory — the primary independent monitoring infrastructure for US election disinformation — was dismantled under political pressure. The AI Governance Monitor tracks both the capability (deepfakes at scale) and the governance vacuum (no disclosure law, SIO gone); the Democratic Integrity monitor tracks the electoral environment they are being deployed into.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: The SIO dismantlement is itself a successful information operation targeting disinformation research infrastructure. The combination of deepfake capability + regulatory vacuum + destroyed monitoring capacity creates the conditions for the first US federal election cycle with unmonitored AI-generated political advertising at scale.

version_history.reason: Reuters confirmed AI deepfakes in midterm campaigns

version_history.change: Updated — SIO dismantlement added as second structural component
version_history.reason: SIO dismantlement creates structural detection gap for midterms; materially changes the risk assessment
version_history.prior_value: Deepfakes in campaigns without disclosure law

flags.title: Military AI procurement two-tier market + autonomous weapons governance vacuum
flags.linkage: The Anthropic-Pentagon injunction (26 March) formally established that the executive branch can designate AI companies as national security supply chain risks for maintaining ethical use conditions. The OpenAI-Pentagon deal (amended 3 March) established the ‘any lawful use’ template. Sen. Gillibrand’s letter revealed DoD rejected guardrails on autonomous weapons. The AI Governance Monitor tracks the procurement architecture; the Strategic Conflict monitor tracks the operational deployment environment those systems enter.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: Our IHL friction analysis (DoD 3000.09 vs. hypersonic engagement timelines; DoD rejection of autonomous weapons guardrails) maps directly to escalation dynamics the conflict monitor tracks. The governance vacuum we document in M08 and M11 becomes operationally relevant when autonomous systems are deployed in active theatres.

version_history.reason: Golden Dome + Anthropic Pentagon injunction

version_history.change: Updated — DoD rejection of autonomous weapons guardrails added (Gillibrand letter)
version_history.reason: Material new evidence on DoD’s explicit refusal to contractualise LAWS restrictions
version_history.prior_value: Military AI procurement and autonomous weapons governance

monitors_involved: Global Environmental Risks & Planetary Boundaries Monitor
flags.title: Hyperscalers as regulated utilities: AI energy infrastructure meets planetary boundaries
flags.linkage: Ratepayer Protection Pledge + PJM BTM rulemaking + EIA pilot survey = three-vector convergence toward treating hyperscalers as regulated energy utilities in a single quarter. $700B capex in 2026; 80% of $2.9T through 2028 still ahead. The AI Governance Monitor tracks the compute infrastructure decisions; the Environmental Risks monitor tracks whether they are compatible with grid stability, energy transition timelines, and planetary boundary constraints.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: We track who controls compute infrastructure and how energy lock-in creates competitive moats. The Environmental Risks monitor provides the planetary-boundary context. The Ratepayer Pledge converting hyperscalers into utility-style entities is the structural event that connects both monitors: it simultaneously constrains AI infrastructure investment returns and forces engagement with the energy transition.

version_history.reason: EIA + hyperscaler PPA dominance

version_history.change: Updated — Ratepayer Protection Pledge + PJM rulemaking added as concrete regulatory convergence
version_history.reason: Three-vector regulatory convergence in Q1 2026 materially changes the assessment from ‘projected’ to ‘in progress’
version_history.prior_value: AI energy demand as environmental stress

flags.title: EU AI Act Omnibus 28 April trilogue target: European AI regulatory sovereignty at a deadline
flags.linkage: The Omnibus trilogue (opened 26 March; target 28 April agreement) is a European strategic autonomy event as much as a compliance event. The AI Act is Europe’s primary instrument for asserting AI governance sovereignty; the Omnibus is the mechanism for making that instrument workable. The EU AI Act Omnibus and the Standards Vacuum we track in M09 are directly relevant to the European Strategic Autonomy monitor’s tracking of European independence in technology governance.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: The Standards Vacuum (124 days to deadline, no harmonised standards in OJ) is not just a compliance problem — it is a strategic vulnerability. European companies facing compliance obligations without technical standards are disadvantaged relative to US labs with no equivalent framework. The 28 April deadline is the moment when European regulatory sovereignty either consolidates or fractures.

version_history.reason: Omnibus vote + AMI Labs + Standards Vacuum

version_history.change: Updated — 28 April trilogue target elevated to acute; White House federal preemption call as contrast frame
version_history.reason: Omnibus now in trilogue with specific date; White House Framework provides the adversarial context
version_history.prior_value: EU AI regulatory framework as strategic autonomy instrument

flags.title: OpenAI $852B valuation at 60x revenue: AI investment concentration and systemic financial risk
flags.linkage: OpenAI’s $122B round at $852B valuation (60x revenue) closed 31 March — the most extreme valuation/revenue ratio in tech history. SoftBank, sovereign wealth funds, and institutional allocators are all now exposed. The AI Governance Monitor tracks the strategic implications of this investment concentration; the Macro Monitor tracks debt, credit, market structure, and systemic risk. If confidence breaks in the AI valuation thesis, the macro implications are now large enough to be systemic.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: We track investment signals for what they reveal about strategic priorities. The retail investor tranche ($3B via banking channels) opening this round marks a potential democratisation of AI concentration risk. The Macro Monitor provides the financial stability framing for what a correction at this scale would mean for the AI infrastructure buildout.

version_history.reason: OpenAI $120B+ rolling round; $14B projected loss; hyperscaler capex $750B

version_history.change: Updated — round confirmed closed at $852B (60x revenue); retail investor tranche noted as new structural element
version_history.reason: Round close confirmed; retail participation materially changes systemic risk profile
